cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Okay, here's a good one to start with.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: You call DELETE on the same order twice, and the first call comes back 200, but the second one comes back 404.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: So your teammate says, see, different responses, which means DELETE isn't idempotent.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: It sounds convincing.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: But is your teammate actually right?

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: And if you just said yes, well, this is the episode for you.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: I'm Leo.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And I'm Maya, and this is the topic that opens almost every SDET interview I've ever run, which is API testing.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Because here's the thing, if you can only test through the browser, you're really just testing the paint on the house, when APIs are the plumbing underneath.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: And interviewers love it because it's so fast to probe, one question about status codes and they know your depth in thirty seconds, so let's build that depth.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Start with why API tests even matter, because why not just do everything through the UI?

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Three reasons, and this is a spoken answer you should have ready.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: The first is speed, because an API test runs in milliseconds while a UI test has to spin up a whole browser.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: The second is stability, so no flaky selectors, no waiting for spinners, and far less flakiness overall.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And the third is precision, because when a UI test fails, the bug could be anywhere in the stack, but when an API test fails, you know exactly which endpoint and which field.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And that's why they sit in the middle of the test pyramid, more of them than UI tests, fewer than unit tests.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Okay, so the alphabet of this whole topic is HTTP methods, GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE, and everyone knows the one-liners.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: So where do people actually get burned?

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Idempotency, and that word decides who gets the follow-up round, so let's really nail it.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: An operation is idempotent if calling it once, or calling it fifty times, leaves the server in the same state.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Okay, give me the physical version.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Think of a light switch that only turns off.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: You press it once and the light's off, and then you press it twenty more times...

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: and it's still off.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: The state of the room didn't change after that first press, and that's idempotent.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Now compare that to dropping a letter in a mailbox, because if you drop two letters, there are two letters in the box, and that's POST.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: So POST creates, and it's not idempotent, because two calls means two resources, which is exactly why double-clicking a place order button can charge you twice.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Exactly, and that's a genuinely great example to use in an interview, because it shows you know why this matters.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Now for the rest of the family.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: GET retrieves, and it's idempotent, it's safe, and it's cacheable.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: PUT replaces the entire resource, so it's idempotent too, because replacing the whole thing with the same thing twice is still the same thing.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: DELETE is idempotent as well.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And then PATCH is the tricky one, because it's a partial update, and it's not necessarily idempotent.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Wait, hold on, PUT versus PATCH, say that again slower, because I know candidates blur these two.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Okay, picture a whiteboard with your profile written on it.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: PUT erases the whole board and rewrites everything from scratch, so if you send the same PUT twice, the board reads identically.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: PATCH walks up and edits one line, say just the email.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Now, if the patch says set the email to this value, doing it twice is fine, but if the patch says add five dollars to the balance, well, do that twice and you've got a different state.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And that's why PATCH is only sometimes idempotent, because it depends on what the patch actually does.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Got it.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: So now back to the cold open, DELETE twice, 200 and then 404, and my teammate says different responses means it's not idempotent.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And your teammate is wrong, and this is the exact nuance interviewers fish for.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Idempotency is about the server's state, not the response you get back.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: After the first DELETE, the order is gone, and after the second DELETE, the order is...

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: still gone.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Same end state.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: That 404 just means there's nothing here to delete, which is honest, because the switch is already off.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: So the sentence to memorize is, idempotent means same state after one call or many, not same response.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Love it.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Okay, status codes, give me the map, but the version for the ear.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: There are four families you care about.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: The 200s mean success, the 300s mean redirect, as in go look over there, the 400s mean the client messed up, so you sent something wrong, and the 500s mean the server messed up, meaning you did nothing wrong, the backend just fell over.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And as a tester, that fourth-versus-fifth distinction is triage gold, because a 4xx points at the request while a 5xx points at the service.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: And now we're in trap corner, because the single most asked status question in QA interviews is 401 versus 403.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: So here's the analogy that makes it stick, and it's a nightclub bouncer.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: A 401 means the bouncer has no idea who you are, maybe you didn't show ID, or your ID is fake or expired, so you're not authenticated.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: A 403 means the bouncer checked your ID, knows exactly who you are...

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: and you're still not on the list.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: So you're authenticated, but not permitted.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: So 401 is the bouncer asking who you are, and 403 is the bouncer saying I know exactly who you are, and the answer is still no.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And that's the whole answer, delivered in one breath.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Two more worth having ready, though.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: There's 429, which means too many requests, so you've hit a rate limit, and by the way, that's something you should be testing on purpose.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And there's 409, which means conflict, and the classic case is trying to create a user with an email that already exists, where the right answer is often a 409, not a 500, and knowing that shows design maturity.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Okay, here's where I want to push, because I've seen this in real code review.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Someone writes a test that calls the endpoint, asserts the status equals 200, and calls it done.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Then the interviewer asks what you validate in an API test, and if your answer is just the status code, you're finished.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: So what's the real checklist?

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: There are five layers, and I'd literally count them on your fingers in the room.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: First is the body, the actual values and the types, so is the price really 49.99, and is it a number, not the string 49.99, because type bugs are sneaky.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Second is the structure, meaning does the response match the expected schema, with all the required fields present and nothing missing.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Third is headers, so content type, caching, and security headers.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Fourth is response time, because is it inside the SLA, the service level agreement, and a correct answer that takes nine seconds is still a failing test.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And fifth, the one that separates seniors from juniors, is side effects.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Meaning the API said it created something, but did it actually create anything?

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Right, because a 201 from a POST is a claim, not proof.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: So you follow up with a GET and confirm the resource really persisted with the right data, which is trust, but verify.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And if an interviewer hears you say you verify the side effect with a follow-up GET, they relax visibly.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: I've watched it happen.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Let's talk test design, because how would you test this endpoint is basically guaranteed to come up.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: So say I've got POST slash users, walk me through it.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Positive cases first, so valid input, expect the 201, verify the body, and verify persistence.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Then comes the negative catalog, and this maps beautifully onto the status codes we just did.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Missing or malformed required fields, expect a 400 or a 422.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: A bad or expired token, that's a 401.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: A valid token but the wrong role, like a regular user hitting an admin endpoint, that's a 403.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Asking for a user that doesn't exist gives you a 404, and creating a duplicate gives you a 409.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And then boundary values, so empty strings, maximum lengths, zero, and negative numbers.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: And what about the ones people forget?

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Pagination, so what happens on page zero, or a page past the end.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Rate limiting, as in do you actually get the 429, and does it recover afterward.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And concurrency, which is two requests updating the same resource at the same instant, and mentioning concurrency unprompted is a real senior signal.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Okay, auth, because none of these tests run if you can't get in the door.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Give me the landscape.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: There are three tiers, from simplest to richest.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: First there's an API key, which is a static secret in a header, kind of like a building keycard.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Then there are Bearer tokens, usually a JWT, a JSON Web Token, which is three parts separated by dots, a header, a payload, and a signature.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: The payload carries claims, so things like exp, which is the expiry time, sub, the subject, meaning who you are, and often your roles.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And then there's OAuth 2.0, the full framework for delegated access, which is how log in with Google actually works.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: And the interview favorite here, and I've been asked this twice, is token expiry.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: So your suite runs forty minutes, but your token dies at thirty.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: What do you do?

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Well, the wrong answer is to log in before every test, because that hammers the auth server and slows everything down.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: The right answer is to get the token once, in a setup step or a fixture, cache it, share it across tests, and refresh it only when it expires, either by checking that exp claim, or by catching a 401 and re-authenticating once.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Because login per test is the mark of a suite that was never reviewed.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Last big idea, schema validation versus contract testing, and I'll be honest, for a long time I thought those were the same thing.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Most people do, which is exactly why it gets asked.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Schema validation checks the shape of one response, in isolation, so right fields, right types, on my side.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Contract testing, and the big tool here is Pact, verifies the agreement between two services, so the consumer writes down exactly what it depends on, and the provider's build replays that contract and fails if it breaks.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: So schema is me checking my own mailbox, and contract is both parties signing the lease, so if the landlord changes the terms, an alarm goes off.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Perfect.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And the payoff line for the interview is that contract tests catch breaking changes between services without needing a full end-to-end integration environment, so they're consumer-driven, fast, and cheap.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: That's the whole pitch of Pact in one sentence.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: All right, time for the sixty-second recap.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: If the interviewer asks you to explain idempotency...

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Say that it means the same server state after one call or many, and the response can differ.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: DELETE twice gives you a 200 and then a 404, but the resource is equally gone either way.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Then name POST as the non-idempotent one, because two calls means two resources.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: And if they ask about 401 versus 403...

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Go straight to the bouncer.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: A 401 means the server doesn't know who you are, so not authenticated, and a 403 means it knows exactly who you are and you're still not allowed, so authenticated, but not permitted.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: And if they ask what you validate in an API test...

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Never just the status code.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: It's body values and types, schema, headers, response time against the SLA, and the side effect, meaning you follow the POST with a GET and prove it persisted.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Do that, and the API round is yours.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Next episode we're covering test pyramids, and why your manager keeps saying the word flaky.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Go get the offer.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: See you there.
